title-group.article-title: Association between depression and three key COVID-19-related outcomes: The SHARE study

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Acute Infectious Disease Control, The Affiliated Wuxi Center for Disease Control and Prevention of Nanjing Medical University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China
bDepartment of Pharmacy, Nantong First People’s Hospital, Second Affiliated Hospital of Nantong University, Nantong, Jiangsu, China

dDepartment of Acute Infectious Disease Control and Immunization Program, Wuzhong Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Suzhou, Jiangsu, China
eDepartment of Medical Administration, Suzhou Industrial Park Medical and Health Management Center, Suzhou, Jiangsu, China
fDepartment of Clinical Research Center, Jiangnan University Medical Center, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China

Yuan Shen wxcdcshy@163.com Department of Acute Infectious Disease Control, the Affiliated Wuxi Center for Disease Control and Prevention of Nanjing Medical University, Wuxi Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214023, China
Kaixin Zhang m15044076625_1@163.com Department of Clinical Research Center, Jiangnan University Medical Center, Wuxi No. 2 People’s Hospital, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214002, China

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: This study investigated the potential associations between preexisting depression and COVID-19 vaccination, SARS-CoV-2 infection risk, and long COVID among middle-aged and older Europeans, based on the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE). A total of 31,115 participants were included to assess the relationship between depression and COVID-19 vaccination 11,146 to evaluate depression-related SARS-CoV-2 infection risk, and 11,142 to study the impact of depression on the occurrence of long COVID. Multivariable logistic model was performed and odds ratios (ORs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were estimated. Compared to non-depressed individuals, those with depression were 0.72 times less likely to receive the COVID-19 vaccine (95% CI: 0.67–0.77), had a 1.15 times higher risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection (95% CI: 1.02–1.30), and were 1.21 times more likely to develop long COVID (95% CI: 1.05–1.38), with these associations strengthening as the severity of depression increased. Preexisting depression might be linked to reduced COVID-19 vaccination odds and an elevated risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection and long COVID, although further high-quality prospective studies with larger samples are needed to confirm the findings.

kwd-group.title: KEYWORDS
kwd-group.kwd: survey of health, ageing, and retirement in Europe

funding-group.funding-statement: This work was supported by Wuxi Development Discipline Construction Project [NO. FZXK2021010].

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The COVID-19 pandemic caused over 776 million infections and 7.07 million deaths globally,1 with at least 10% of survivors experiencing long COVID,2–5 leading to significant health and economic burdens.6–8

Depression could influence people’s health by affecting their behavioral choices, as well as by altering immune function and triggering chronic inflammation.9,10 The same might apply to COVID-19-related outcomes. On the one hand, several previous studies indicated that lower vaccination rates among depressed individuals,11–13 while others suggested comparable or slightly higher rates.14–16 Vaccination rates in individuals with depression remain controversial. In addition, few studies specifically explored vaccination rates among depressed populations aged 50 and above. On the other hand, although some research has found that preexisting psychological distress such as depression was linked to higher risks of SARS-CoV-2 infection, severe symptoms, and long COVID, possibly due to inflammation and immune dysregulation.17–20 However, studies examining the association between depression and SARS-CoV-2 infection or long COVID were still limited. To our knowledge, vaccination might rectify dysregulated immune or inflammatory responses and reduced the risk of long COVID as well as symptom severity.21–24 However, there was also few studies analyzing the interaction between vaccination and depression.

This study utilized data from the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE) to prospectively investigate the relationships between depression and three key COVID-19-related outcomes (COVID-19 vaccination status, SARS-CoV-2 infection risk, and the occurrence of long COVID) among adults aged ≥50 years in European populations. Additionally, this study also aimed to explore the joint impact of depression and COVID-19 vaccination on SARS-CoV-2 infection and long COVID.

sec.title: Study design and sample
sec.p: The present study was conducted among European aged ≥50 years based on the SHARE study, an ongoing prospective, longitudinal population-based study in Europe. Details of the study design have been published elsewhere.25 Briefly, SHARE is a biennial survey (since 2004) of adults aged 50 and older from 27 European countries and Israel. All cohabiting partners (regardless of age) are interviewed, and participants from prior waves form the longitudinal sample. The current study utilized data from Wave 8 (2019–2020, including the 2020 SHARE Corona Survey 1) and Wave 9 (2021–2022, including the 2021 SHARE Corona Survey 2) of the SHARE study (Supplementary text).26–28

The study initially included 44,775 participants aged 50 and older with Euro-Depression information in Wave 8 as the baseline population. Following exclusion of ineligible participants and those with incomplete data, the study comprised 31,115 individuals for evaluating depression’s association with COVID-19 vaccination uptake 11,146 for assessing depression-related SARS-CoV-2 infection risk, and 11,142 for examining depressive effects on long COVID incidence (Figure 1). Figure 1.
Flow chart of participants’ selection. SHARE, Survey of Health and Retirement in Europe; SARS-CoV-2, Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2; COVID-19, Coronavirus disease 2019.

sec.title: Exposure: depression
sec.p: Depression was assessed using two measures from the SHARE Wave 8 database (sharew8_GV). First, a binary classification (eurodcat) distinguished individuals meeting diagnostic criteria for depression (score ≥4 on the European Depression Scale, coded as 1) from those below this threshold (score <4, coded as 0).29,30 Second, depressive symptom severity was evaluated using the continuous ‘eurod’ variable (range: 0–12), with higher scores reflecting greater severity.29,31

sec.title: Outcome: COVID-19 vaccination, SARS-CoV-2 infection and long COVID
sec.p: COVID-19 vaccination status data were collected by asking the participants whether they received the COVID-19 vaccine(s) in the past 12 months in SHARE Corona Survey 2. SARS-CoV-2 infection status was assessed through two survey questions: Have you or someone close to you tested positive for COVID-19? and Who tested positive? Please specify their relationship to you. Only participants reporting their own positive test result(s) were included in the analysis. The analysis of depression’s impact on long COVID occurrence was based on confirmed COVID-19 cases. To gather information about long COVID, participants were asked if they experienced any long-lasting or persistent effects due to COVID-19 infection, such as fatigue, cough, loss of taste or smell, etc in SHARE Corona Survey 2. If one or more of these symptoms were present, the participant was considered to have long COVID. Long COVID is an infection-associated chronic condition that occurs after SARS-CoV-2 infection and is present for at least 3 months as a continuous, relapsing and remitting, or progressive disease state that affects one or more organ systems.32

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The analysis of this study included the impact of depression on COVID-19 vaccination, the likelihood of depression patients contracting COVID-19 and developing long COVID, as well as the combined effect of depression and COVID-19 vaccination on the occurrence of long COVID. Continuous variables were presented as mean ± SD or median (interquartile range) and were compared using appropriate statistical tests. Categorical variables were expressed as number (percentage) and compared using χ2 test.

The multivariate logistic model was applied to evaluate the association between depression and COVID-19 vaccination, SARS-CoV-2 infection, long COVID, along with the joint effect of depression and COVID-19 vaccination on the long COVID development, respectively. Odds ratios (ORs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were calculated. Tests for linear trend in risk across Euro-scale quartiles were performed using the category as continuous variables. ORs and 95% CIs for 1-unit increment of Euro-scale were also calculated. Furthermore, we performed additional analyses. Considering that participants without SARS-CoV-2 testing might also have been infected, we included those with COVID-19 symptoms in our analysis to examine the impact of depression on either SARS-CoV-2 positivity or COVID-19 symptoms. After excluding uninfected participants, we reassessed the association between depression and long COVID. And, whether vaccination modified the relationship of depression with both SARS-CoV-2 infection and long COVID was further investigated. Statistical significance was defined as a p-value less than 0.01, and data analysis was performed using SAS statistical software (version 9.4).

sec.title: Baseline characteristics
sec.p:
The depressed group showed statistically significant trends compared to the non-depressed group: they were older, had a higher proportion of females, experienced a higher rate of worsened health conditions from pre-pandemic to pandemic periods, higher ADL score and had a lower rate of history of smoking, alcohol consumption in the past three months and pneumonia vaccination. These characteristics became more pronounced with increasing Euro-Depression scale scores. Similar characteristics were also observed in the analysis concerning the relationship between depression and SARS-CoV-2 positive (Table 1 and Supplementary Table I).Table 1.
Baseline characteristics of the study participants (exposure: depression, outcome: COVID-19 vaccination).

Abbreviations: COVID = Coronavirus disease; ADL = Activities of daily living.
Continuous variables are expressed as mean ± SD or median (interquartile range), and compared using student t tests, Satterhwaite t’ tests, F tests or Wilcoxon rank-sum tests as appropriate.
Categorical variables are expressed as number (percent) and compared using χ2 tests.

sec.title: Depression and COVID-19 vaccination, SARS-CoV-2 positive, long COVID
sec.p:
Depressed individuals had 0.72 times (95% CI: 0.67–0.77) lower likelihood of receiving COVID-19 vaccination compared to non-depressed individuals (Multivariable-adjusted model). Moreover, there was a notable trend indicating that as depression severity increased, the probability of vaccination decreased (p for trend <.001). Each one-point increase in the EURO-D scale was associated with a 7% decrease in the odds of vaccination (OR 0.93, 95% CI: 0.91–0.94) (Table 2). Compared to non-depressed individuals, patients with depression exhibited 1.15 times higher odds of SARS-CoV-2 infection and 1.21 times higher likelihood of developing long COVID (95% CI: 1.02–1.30 and 1.05–1.38, respectively; Multivariable-adjusted model). Higher severity of depression was associated with greater infection risk (p for trend = .01) and increased probability of long COVID development (p for trend < .001). Each one-point increase on the EURO-D scale corresponded to a 3% elevation in the odds of infection and an 5% rise in the odds of long COVID. After including participants reporting COVID-19 symptoms and excluding uninfected individuals, the associations between depression and SARS-CoV-2 positive/COVID-19 symptoms as well as long COVID showed similar results (Tables 3 and 4).Table 2.
Odds ratios and 95% confidence intervals of COVID-19 vaccination by degree of depression.
DepressionEURO-D scale
NoYesQ1Q2Q3Q4p for trendEach one-point increase
COVID-19 vaccination
Cases (%)18805 (81.64)6140 (75.98)5775 (81.20)5424 (82.63)7606 (81.28)6140 (75.98)–24945 (80.17)

Multivariable-adjusted1.000.72 (0.67–0.77)1.001.09 (0.99–1.20)0.96 (0.88–1.05)0.72 (0.66–0.79) <0.0010.93 (0.91–0.94)
Abbreviations: EURO-D = Euro-Depression scale; COVID = Coronavirus disease.
Quartiles of EURO-D scale were 0, 1, 2–3, ≥4.
Multivariable-adjusted model included age, sex, history of smoking, drinking frequency in recent 3 months, health status change pre/post COVID-19, influenza vaccination, pneumonia vaccination and ADL scale.Table 3.

sec.title: The effects of depression and COVID-19 vaccination on SARS-CoV-2 positive and long COVID
sec.p:
The joint effects showed that both unvaccinated non-depressed individuals and unvaccinated depressed individuals were observed stronger statistical association with SARS-CoV-2 positivity and long COVID. Vaccination did not significantly modify the relationships of depression with SARS-CoV-2 infection and long COVID (Table 5).Table 5.
The effects of depression and COVID-19 vaccination on the risk of SARS-CoV-2 positive and long COVID.
Study outcomes

Combined effect
+–Ref/Ref/

––3.02 (2.63–3.47) < 0.0012.95 (2.53–3.45) < 0.001
–+2.89 (2.37–3.52) < 0.0013.05 (2.46–3.77) < 0.001

COVID-19 vaccination * Depression#/0.110/0.228
Abbreviations: SARS-CoV-2 = Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2; COVID = Corona Virus Disease; OR = Odds ratio; CI = Confidence Interval.
Multivariable-adjusted model included age, sex, history of smoking, drinking frequency in recent 3 months, health status change pre/post COVID-19, influenza vaccination, pneumonia vaccination and ADL scale. # The interaction term COVID-19 vaccination * Depression was treated as a variable in the multivariable-adjusted model.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In this multicenter prospective study of European adults aged ≥50 years, participants with prior depression demonstrated lower odds of COVID-19 vaccination uptake, elevated contracting COVID-19 risk, and increased susceptibility to long COVID compared to those without depression. The association became stronger with the increasing severity of depression. To the best of our knowledge, our study was the first to further explore the combined effects of depression and COVID-19 vaccination on the risk of developing long COVID in this population, though the analyses were merely conducted through cross-sectional study.

Previous research showed that psychological factors were associated with increased susceptibility to viral respiratory infections and more severe symptom manifestations.33 A prospective cohort study by Ayling et al. revealed that during the early stages of the COVID-19 pandemic, greater psychological distress, including depression, was significantly related to subsequent self-reported SARS-CoV-2 infection and more frequent and severe symptoms.17 A cohort study utilizing the UK Biobank data found that preexisting psychiatric disorders such as depression were linked to elevated risks of COVID-19 infection.18 These findings aligned with the observations in our study. The possible mechanisms by which SARS-CoV-2 contributed to long COVID included abnormalities in coagulation, the microbiome, and neuroimmune functions.3,34 Research indicated that inflammation and immune dysregulation might link psychological distress with long COVID.19 Bauer et al. showed that mental disorders and inflammation might drive each other in a bidirectional loop, where depression promoted inflammatory responses, and inflammation also exacerbated depression and other neuropsychiatric conditions.35 Currently, studies on the increased risk of long COVID in individuals with preexisting depressive symptoms primarily focused on healthcare workers and patients with medical records.19,20,36–38 This study, which targeted the European community population aged 50 and older participating in the SHARE study, also found a significantly higher risk of long COVID among those with depressive symptoms. This enhanced the generalizability of the study’s conclusions.

The COVID-19 vaccination status of individuals with depressive symptoms remained controversial. Hassan et al. found that people with recurrent major depression had a higher rate of receiving at least one dose of the COVID-19 vaccine, but the vaccination rate among this group declined rapidly afterward. Moreover, the findings were limited to Greater Manchester.14 Hui Huang et al. and N A Uvais’s cross-sectional studies of psychiatric hospital (clinic) patients,11,13 as well as Kimberly H Nguyen et al.‘s cross-sectional study of U.S. adults,12 supported that individuals with depressive symptoms had a lower COVID-19 vaccination rate compared to the general population, which was consistent with our findings. This might be related to individuals with depressive symptoms being more likely to support COVID-19 vaccine misinformation,39 due to concern about the potential side effects of the COVID-19 vaccine on their symptoms and medications.40 Research by Hong Cai et al. suggested that the stigma associated with mental illnesses such as depression was also a potential barrier for patients in seeking healthcare.41 Our study focused on the European community population aged 50 and above, exploring the vaccination status of individuals with depressive symptoms in these vulnerable groups based on different waves data from the SHARE study, with a clear temporal sequence. Although the joint effects analysis revealed that the impact of depression alone was smaller than that of being unvaccinated alone, given the multifaceted influence of depression (including its effects on vaccination behavior and immune regulation), depression remains a risk factor worthy of further investigation.

This study of community-dwelling depressed adults aged ≥50 (a vulnerable yet often overlooked demographic) across 27 European countries and Israel made significant progress beyond prior research that either examined mental health broadly or focused on specific institutionalized populations (e.g., psychiatric inpatients). Using EURO-D scale’s continuous measures, surpassing binary classification limitations. Unvaccinated depressed patients might face higher risks, guiding precision interventions and personalized care. The findings underscored the need to integrate mental health support into pandemic responses to improve healthcare engagement in this population and mitigated long-term impacts, while establishing a foundation for exploring biological mechanisms and the effectiveness of integrated mental-physical health interventions. Certainly, there were some limitations. First, the cross-sectional design of analyzing joint depression-vaccination associations with long COVID precluded causal inference. Second, while our findings provided insights into European populations aged 50 years and older, the applicability to other groups or geographic regions required further investigation. Third, the analysis of depression’s association with long COVID was limited to participants with available SARS-CoV-2 test results in SHARE Corona Survey 2. Fourth, the study did not find a significant moderating effect of vaccination on the association between depression and infection or long COVID. However, the differences between subgroups suggested that well-designed, large-sample prospective studies are needed to further explore this. Fifth, there might be unmeasured confounding factors that lead to bias in the estimation of results. Finally, as in prior research,33,42 self-reported vaccination and long COVID data might be susceptible to reporting inaccuracies. During the SHARE Corona Survey 2, participants were required to retrospectively report suspected long COVID symptoms occurring within one year after the SHARE Corona Survey 1, potentially inducing recall bias and inflating prevalence estimates.

In conclusion, among the participants in this study, preexisting depression might be associated with lower odds of COVID-19 vaccination and higher incidence of SARS-CoV-2 infection and long COVID. More well-designed large-sample prospective studies are required to verify these findings.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: This paper uses data from SHARE waves 8 and 9 (DOIs: 10.6103/SHARE.w8.800, 10.6103/SHARE.w8ca.800 and 10.6103/SHARE.w9ca800). Börsch-Supan, A. (2022). Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE) Wave 8. Release version: 8.0.0. SHARE-ERIC. Data set. DOI:10.6103/SHARE.w8.800. Börsch-Supan, A. (2022). Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE) Wave 8. COVID-19 Survey 1. Release version: 8.0.0. SHARE-ERIC. Data set. DOI:10.6103/SHARE.w8ca.800. Börsch-Supan, A. (2022). Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE) Wave 9. COVID-19 Survey 2. Release version: 8.0.0. SHARE-ERIC. Data set. DOI:10.6103/SHARE.w9ca.800.

bio.p: Yujun Chen, Deputy Chief Physician, serves as the deputy director of the acute infectious disease control department at the Wuxi Center for Disease Control and Prevention. He is a key member of Wuxi’s public health development discipline, with a primary research focus on acute infectious diseases.

Yuan Shen Chief Physician, is the deputy director of the Wuxi Center for Disease Control and Prevention, in charge of the acute infectious disease control department. Doctor Shen is also an associate professor and master’s supervisor at the School of Public Health Nanjing Medical University, with a research focus on infectious disease epidemiology. Doctor Shen has been the discipline leader of Public Health Development in Wuxi since 2021. As the head of this discipline, Doctor Shen has been dedicated to the prevention and control of acute infectious diseases and other public health-related work.
Kaixin Zhang, Assistant Research Fellow, is currently affiliated with Wuxi Second People’s Hospital (Medical Center of Jiangnan University). Her main research focus is on disease epidemiology.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

sec.title: Ethical statement
sec.p: The study adhered to the ethical principles outlined in the World Medical Declaration of Helsinki and received approval from the University of Valencia’s Ethics Committee for Human Research (register code 1510464).

sec.title: Supplementary material
sec.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/21645515.2025.2551930
